items.name: Talk Talk — Spirit of Eden
items.reason: Both bands abandoned conventional rock structures for ambient, post-rock soundscapes, with Mark Hollis dissolving melody into atmosphere years before Radiohead made alienation feel devotional.

items.name: AMM — AMMMusic
items.reason: The British improv collective treated silence and texture like composition, laying the groundwork for the spacious anti-song logic that makes OK Computer feel haunted rather than merely experimental.

items.name: Koyaanisqatsi
items.reason: Godfrey Reggio's city symphony turns technology into religious spectacle, the same mix of awe and dread Radiohead found in motorways, screens, and systems too large to love us back.

items.name: Ron Fricke — Chronos
items.reason: Fricke strips dialogue away until civilization looks like a time-lapse nervous system, a pure visual analogue for OK Computer's sense of human life being swallowed by patterns.

items.name: Brasília
items.reason: Brazil's planned capital embodies the album's core anxiety: utopian design that looks immaculate from the air and feels estranging, even lonely, once actual humans arrive.

items.name: Lucio Costa
items.reason: Costa's airplane-shaped master plan for Brasília reads like OK Computer in civic form: elegant systems thinking that accidentally makes everyday life feel eerily inhuman.

items.name: Andrei Tarkovsky — Stalker
items.reason: Tarkovsky turns rusted machinery and spiritual exhaustion into a pilgrimage, matching Radiohead's vision of progress as slow apocalypse rather than triumphant modernity.

items.name: Arkady Strugatsky — Roadside Picnic
items.reason: The novel behind Stalker imagines technology as radioactive mystery, cosmic leftovers that humans can exploit but never understand, exactly the frequency OK Computer hums on.

items.name: György Ligeti — Lux Aeterna
items.reason: Ligeti turns voices into an alien weather system, the same trick Radiohead uses when guitars and melodies dissolve into dread, beauty, and loss of human scale.

items.name: Hilliard Ensemble — Morimur
items.reason: Bach and Pärt fold centuries together until sacred music feels unmoored from time, which is the same uncanny temporal drift hiding inside OK Computer's digital paranoia.

items.name: Miles Davis — Bitches Brew
items.reason: The anchor: both albums shocked their audiences by using the studio to melt familiar forms into something atmospheric, disorienting, and permanently future-facing.

items.name: Teo Macero
items.reason: Macero's tape-splice production made editing itself feel compositional, a direct precursor to how Radiohead and Nigel Godrich broke rock apart inside the studio.
